ex_zh: The old man's belligerence was a rusty armor he had worn so long he had forgotten there was nothing underneath but tired bones.

def_en: severe punishment or criticism, especially physical discipline
def_zh: receive chastisement, physical chastisement, divine chastisement
quote_en: "In his other life Maurice had received chastisement for filling a younger eye with sand." – The narrator explaining why Maurice hesitates after accidentally getting sand in Percival’s eyes, as memory of adult punishment briefly restrains him (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'chastisement' – formal, almost biblical, implying deserved punishment – evokes the adult world of discipline that the boys have left behind. Maurice does not refrain from cruelty because he is kind; he refrains because he remembers being punished for similar cruelty 'in his other life', before the island. The word 'chastisement' suggests that civilisation is internalised not through moral development but through fear of consequences. When the consequences vanish, so does the restraint. This is a bleak view of human nature: we behave not because we are good but because we are afraid of being caught. Golding uses 'chastisement' to show how fragile moral behaviour is when it is based only on punishment. Maurice will eventually overcome this hesitation; the memory of chastisement fades, and he joins Jack's tribe. The word also echoes the novel's theological undertones: the boys are being chastised by their own nature, punished by the beast within.
ex_en: The memory of parental chastisement faded quickly on the island, leaving nothing to restrain Roger’s cruelty but the ghost of a broken law, and ghosts, the boys were learning, could be ignored.
ex_zh: The headmaster's chastisement was legendary: six strokes of the cane, each one a syllable in a sentence no boy ever wanted to read again.

def_en: an invasion or attack, especially a sudden or brief one
def_zh: enemy incursion, last incursion, military incursion
quote_en: "Perhaps food had appeared where at the last incursion there had been none; bird droppings, insects perhaps, any of the strewn detritus of landward life." – The narrator describing the tiny transparent scavengers that come in with each wave, searching for food (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'incursion' – a military term for a raid into enemy territory – is applied to the tide's advance. Each wave is an 'incursion', a brief invasion of the dry sand by the sea. This word choice militarises the natural world, turning the harmless rise and fall of the tide into a pattern of attack and retreat. The scavengers that ride in on the incursion are like advance scouts, searching for food in the newly conquered territory. Henry, watching them, feels a 'sense of mastery' as he controls their movements. The word 'incursion' thus connects the small boy's play to the larger patterns of violence on the island. Every incursion is a rehearsal for the raid on Ralph's shelter, for the theft of Piggy's glasses, for the final hunt. The sea's incursions are natural and harmless; the human incursions are not. Golding uses this word to suggest that aggression is woven into the fabric of existence, from the smallest wave to the largest war.
ex_en: Each incursion of the tide brought tiny sea creatures to Henry’s feet, giving him a fleeting sense of power over life and death, a god of a single rock pool.
ex_zh: The army's incursion across the border was swift and silent, a knife slipped between ribs before the sleeper could dream of a wound.

def_en: waste or debris of any kind; loose material produced by disintegration
def_zh: strewn detritus, organic detritus, industrial detritus
quote_en: "Perhaps food had appeared where at the last incursion there had been none; bird droppings, insects perhaps, any of the strewn detritus of landward life." – The narrator describing the tiny scavengers that search for edible fragments washed up by the tide (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'detritus' – from the Latin for 'worn away' – refers to the broken, scattered remains of organic matter: dead insects, bird droppings, fragments of leaves. The word carries a sense of decay, of life reduced to scattered pieces. On the beach, the detritus is food for the tiny scavengers. On the island as a whole, the detritus of civilisation – broken spears, shattered conch, the body of Piggy – is all that remains of the boys' brief attempt at order. Golding uses this word to evoke the inevitable disintegration of all systems. The boys' civilisation, like the bird droppings, will become detritus scattered by the tide. The novel's final image is Ralph weeping over the 'end of innocence' – but innocence, too, is a kind of detritus, the broken fragments of a childhood that could not survive the island. The scavengers (the crabs, the flies, the tide) will consume everything.
ex_en: The island floor was littered with the detritus of the boys' failed civilization: broken spears, abandoned coconuts, and the shards of Piggy's glasses, each fragment a tombstone for a lost hope.
ex_zh: After the festival, the field lay covered in detritus: crumpled cups, torn tickets, a single forgotten shoe pointing toward the empty stage.

def_en: a countless or extremely large number
def_zh: myriad of stars, myriad of problems, myriad of tiny teeth
quote_en: "Like a myriad of tiny teeth in a saw, the transparencies came scavenging over the beach." – The narrator describing the countless microscopic sea creatures that swarm over the sand with each wave (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'myriad' – from the Greek for 'ten thousand' – conveys the overwhelming abundance of the tiny scavengers. The simile 'like a myriad of tiny teeth in a saw' transforms the harmless creatures into a cutting, devouring force. This image anticipates the novel's later swarms: the flies that cover the pig's head, the painted savages that swarm across the neck of Castle Rock. The myriad is a formless multitude, a crowd of identical units that together become a single threatening entity. Golding uses this word to suggest the horror of the collective. Individual scavengers are insignificant; a myriad of them is a consuming carpet. Similarly, individual boys – Roger alone, Jack alone – are containable. But the tribe, the myriad of painted savages, is unstoppable. The word 'myriad' also evokes the countless stars in the sky, which bring no comfort to the boys, only the vast indifference of the universe.
ex_en: A myriad of fears plagued the littleuns at night, but the biggest fear of all—the beast—was the one they never named, a shadow that multiplied with every whisper.
ex_zh: A myriad of fireflies lit the marsh, each one a fleeting star with an expiration date of dawn.

def_en: a person without a settled home; a wanderer; also adj. wandering
def_zh: vagrant breeze, vagrant stick, vagrant thoughts
quote_en: "He poked about with a bit of stick, that itself was wave-worn and whitened and a vagrant, and tried to control the motions of the scavengers." – The narrator describing Henry playing at the water's edge, using a piece of driftwood (a 'vagrant' stick) to steer the tiny sea creatures (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'vagrant' – a homeless wanderer – is applied to a piece of driftwood. The stick is a vagrant because it has no fixed place, carried by tides and currents, belonging nowhere. This word resonates deeply with the boys' condition. They are vagrants on the island, castaways with no home, no parents, no destination. The stick, wave-worn and whitened, is an image of what they will become: bleached, eroded, unrecognisable. Henry tries to 'control the motions' of the scavengers with his vagrant stick, but the stick is itself uncontrolled, a wanderer. This irony suggests the limits of mastery. The boys try to control their environment, but they are vagrants within it, subject to the same tides that move the driftwood. Simon, the novel's true vagrant, wanders the jungle alone, speaking to no one, belonging to no tribe. His vagrancy is his wisdom and his doom.
ex_en: Simon was a vagrant among the boys, wandering the jungle alone while the others feasted and danced, a ghost in the margins of their savage celebration.
ex_zh: The vagrant sat on the park bench, his cardboard sign a passport to nowhere, his eyes two coins dropped into the fountain of a city that had stopped wishing.

def_en: a feeling of excitement and elation; thrilling joy
def_zh: fierce exhilaration, sense of exhilaration, wild exhilaration
quote_en: "There was the brilliant world of hunting, tactics, fierce exhilaration, skill; and there was the world of longing and baffled commonsense." – The narrator contrasting Jack's hunting world, full of the thrill of the chase, with Ralph's world of rescue and reason (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'exhilaration' – from the Latin for 'to make cheerful' – captures the addictive pleasure of the hunt. The word 'fierce' modifies it, suggesting that this exhilaration is not gentle joy but an aggressive, almost painful intensity. Jack's world is 'brilliant' – bright, attractive, seductive. Ralph's world, by contrast, is described as 'longing and baffled commonsense' – dull, needy, frustrated. Golding presents the choice between these worlds as almost inevitable. Who would not choose exhilaration over baffled common sense? The novel's tragedy is that exhilaration is temporary and destructive. The hunt gives pleasure, but it also erodes the possibility of rescue. The exhilaration of killing the pig leads to the fire going out, the ship passing by, the hope fading. Golding uses this word to show how desire works: the exhilaration of the moment blinds the boys to the long-term consequences. Jack offers them joy; Ralph offers them duty. Joy wins every time.
ex_en: The exhilaration of the kill was intoxicating to Jack, so much so that he forgot the signal fire and doomed them all, a drunkard stumbling past the only door to safety.
ex_zh: The exhilaration of the first snowfall sent the children tumbling into the white silence, their screams of joy the only color in a world erased.

def_en: a series of words spoken as a magic spell or charm
def_zh: magical incantation, ritual incantation, incantation of address
quote_en: "Percival Wemys Madison, of the Vicarage, Harcourt St. Anthony, lying in the long grass, was living through circumstances in which the incantation of his address was powerless to help him." – The narrator describing Percival's breakdown after the assembly about the beast, as repeating his name and address (his 'incantation') fails to comfort him (Chapter 5)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'incantation' – a magical formula spoken to ward off evil – transforms Percival's learned response to fear (reciting his name and address) into a failed spell. In the world of adults, saying who you are and where you live summons help. On the island, it summons nothing. The word 'incantation' suggests that the boys' civilisation was always a kind of magic, a set of words and rituals that worked only because adults enforced them. Without the adult world, the incantation is just noise. Percival's breakdown marks the death of that old magic. Later, the tribe will create new incantations: 'Kill the pig! Cut her throat! Spill her blood!' These savage spells work not because they are true but because the tribe believes in them. Golding uses 'incantation' to show that all societies, civilised or savage, are built on shared words that have no power except the power we give them. The conch is an incantation; so is the beast.
ex_en: The tribe's chanting became an incantation of savagery, each repetition of 'Kill the pig' pulling them further from humanity, a spell that turned boys into wolves.
ex_zh: The old woman's incantation was a whisper of dried herbs and forgotten Latin, a key to a lock that had rusted shut before her grandmother was born.

def_en: endless or seemingly endless; tediously long
def_zh: interminable dawn, interminable wait, interminable night
quote_en: "An interminable dawn faded the stars out, and at last light, sad and grey, filtered into the shelter." – The narrator as Ralph and the others wait for morning after the twins report seeing the beast on the mountain (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'interminable' – from the Latin for 'not ending' – transforms the natural transition from night to day into an agonising ordeal. A dawn is usually a brief, beautiful threshold; this dawn is 'interminable', stretching out like a punishment. The stars 'fade' slowly, each one a small death of hope. The light that finally arrives is not the golden sunrise of the early chapters but 'sad and grey', a colour of exhaustion and dread. Golding uses 'interminable' to convey the psychological experience of terror: time stretches, each second an eternity. The boys are not waiting for rescue; they are waiting for the beast to come. This interminable dawn is a rehearsal for the novel's darker nights – the nights of Simon's murder, of Piggy's death, of Ralph's flight. Time, once a friend, becomes an enemy that refuses to move.
ex_en: The night after Simon's death felt interminable to Ralph, each moment stretching into an eternity of guilt, the darkness a patient predator outside the fragile shelter.
ex_zh: The interminable rain tapped against the window like a finger on a coffin lid, each drop a second, each second a year.

def_en: a heavy base or slab supporting a statue or column; a square block
def_zh: stone plinth, marble plinth, square plinth
quote_en: "The squareness of the rock allowed a sort of plinth round it, so that to the right, over the lagoon, one could inch along a ledge and turn the corner out of sight." – The narrator describing the Castle Rock's structure as Ralph approaches it alone to look for the beast (Chapter 6)
quote_zh: Golding's architectural noun 'plinth' – the base of a column or statue – gives the natural rock formation a man-made, monumental quality. The Castle Rock is not merely a rock; it has a 'plinth', suggesting that it was designed for something – perhaps a statue of a god, a monument to power. This word foreshadows Jack's occupation of the rock. He will turn it into a fortress, a throne, a place of judgement. The plinth is where the chief sits, where the tribe gathers, where the offering to the beast (the pig's head) might have been placed. Golding uses 'plinth' to elevate the rock from geography to symbol. It is the pedestal of savagery, the base upon which the tribe's tyranny is built. The fact that Ralph can 'inch along a ledge' and turn the corner out of sight suggests that the plinth is also a hiding place, a blind spot – the space where the truth (the dead parachutist) can be concealed.
ex_en: The great pink rock of the Castle Rock served as a natural plinth for Jack's throne, where he sat like an idol judging his tribe, a statue of cruelty carved by no sculptor but fear.
ex_zh: The bronze soldier stood on his granite plinth, staring across a square that had forgotten his name, his sword raised against a war that ended before he was poured.

def_en: thickets or hiding places where animals shelter; concealed places
def_zh: ferny coverts, secret coverts, forest coverts
quote_en: "If you could shut your ears to the slow suck down of the sea and boil of the return, if you could forget how dun and unvisited were the ferry coverts on either side, then there was a chance that you might put the beast out of mind and dream for a while." – The narrator describing the other side of the island as Ralph, Jack, and the hunters search for the beast (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'coverts' – a hunting term for places where game animals hide – applies the vocabulary of the chase to the island's landscape. The 'ferry coverts' are dun-coloured (a dull grey-brown) and 'unvisited', suggesting that no one goes there, not even the boys. These coverts are where the beast might lurk, where the pigs hide, where the darkness gathers. The word evokes the ancient practice of hunting: beaters drive animals from the coverts toward the hunters. On the island, the boys are both hunters and prey. The beast (the dead parachutist) hides in its covert; the boys (the hunters) seek it; but eventually, Ralph will become the hunted, and the coverts will hide him. Golding uses this word to create a world of hiding and seeking, of concealment and discovery. The coverts are the spaces between knowledge and fear, the places where the truth sleeps.
ex_en: The jungle coverts that once hid piglets now hid the painted hunters, waiting to ambush Ralph, each fern a curtain, each shadow a promise of pain.
ex_zh: The deer vanished into the forest coverts, a ghost of brown dissolving into brown, leaving behind only the memory of a tail's white flag.

def_en: dejected and disappointed; having one's spirits dampened
def_zh: crestfallen expression, look crestfallen, utterly crestfallen
quote_en: "'I don't remember this cliff,' said Jack, crestfallen, 'so this must be the bit of the coast I missed.'" – Jack admitting he does not know this part of the island as he, Ralph, and the others search for the beast (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'crestfallen' – literally, with a drooping crest like a defeated bird – captures Jack's rare moment of vulnerability. He has boasted of his hunting knowledge, his familiarity with every corner of the island. Now he must admit he has never seen this cliff. The word 'crestfallen' suggests deflation, the collapse of pride. This is Jack at his most human – disappointed, unsure, almost humble. But the moment passes quickly. Jack's crestfallen admission does not lead to self-reflection; it leads to anger, to a redoubled determination to dominate. Golding uses this word to show that humiliation can go two ways: it can produce empathy, or it can produce rage. Jack chooses rage. The crestfallen boy becomes the tyrant who will never admit weakness again. This brief glimpse of Jack's vulnerability makes his later savagery all the more tragic.
ex_en: Crestfallen after losing the election for chief, Jack retreated into the forest, where bitterness festered into tyranny, a fallen crest that grew into a crown of thorns.
ex_zh: The crestfallen puppy lowered its head, eyes upcast, a small tragedian performing the role of Guilt for an audience of one unsoftened heart.

def_en: stared angrily or sullenly; glared with discontent
def_zh: glowered at him, glowered in response, glowered from across the room
quote_en: "Ralph looked back at Jack, seeing him, infuriatingly, for the first time. 'Jack—that time you went the whole way to the castle rock.' Jack glowered. 'Yes?'" – The narrator as the tension between Ralph and Jack intensifies during their search for the beast (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'glowered' – an angry, sullen stare – captures Jack's non-verbal response to Ralph's question. He does not answer with words; he glowers, a look that communicates hostility without engagement. The word suggests a smouldering anger, a fire banked but not extinguished. Jack's glower is a refusal of the conversation Ralph is trying to have. It is also a warning: I am dangerous, do not push me. This small moment is a pivot point in their relationship. Ralph is trying to build a bridge (remembering their earlier exploration together), but Jack's glower burns it down. From this point, their antagonism becomes open, irreconcilable. Golding uses 'glowered' to show how small gestures – a look, a silence – can be more destructive than words. The glower is the face of unreason, the refusal to engage.
ex_en: Jack glowered at Piggy whenever he spoke, his silent rage promising violence that would eventually come, a storm gathered behind a dam of painted clay.
ex_zh: He glowered at the empty fridge as if it had personally eaten his leftovers, his scowl a dark cloud in the bright kitchen.

def_en: seeming difficult to deal with; intimidating
def_zh: daunting task, daunting prospect, successfully daunting
quote_en: "The word was too good, too bitter, too successfully daunting to be repeated." – The narrator describing Jack's taunt 'Coming?' after daring Ralph to climb the mountain to face the beast (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'daunting' – discouraging through fear or doubt – is applied to a single word, Jack's challenge 'Coming?'. The word is 'successfully daunting', meaning it achieves exactly what Jack intends: it makes Ralph hesitate, it throws down a gauntlet that cannot be ignored. The word 'daunting' captures the psychology of intimidation. A daunting task is not necessarily impossible; it is made to seem impossible by the way it is presented. Jack's single word carries the weight of all his contempt, all his hunger for power. Ralph cannot refuse without seeming a coward, but accepting means climbing the mountain in the dark toward an unknown terror. The word is 'too good, too bitter' – effective because it is cruel. Golding uses 'daunting' to show how language can be weaponised, how a simple question can become a trap. The rest of the novel is, in a sense, the playing out of that daunting challenge.
ex_en: The prospect of climbing the mountain in the dark was daunting, but Ralph refused to let Jack see his fear, his trembling legs hidden by the darkness that also hid the beast.
ex_zh: The first day of school loomed before her, a daunting mountain whose peak was hidden by clouds of unknown teachers and lunch tables with no empty seats.

def_en: not affected or influenced by something; unable to be penetrated
def_zh: impervious to fear, impervious stone, impervious surface
quote_en: "So they sat, the rocking, tapping, impervious Roger and Ralph, fuming; round them the close sky was loaded with stars, save where the mountain punched up a hole of blackness." – The narrator describing Ralph and Roger waiting for Jack to return from scouting the beast on the mountain (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'impervious' – from the Latin for 'not allowing passage' – describes both Roger and Ralph in this tense, silent moment. They are 'impervious' to each other, sealed off, unable to connect. Roger, who feels nothing, is impervious to fear, to guilt, to the humanity that connects people. Ralph, trapped in his own anxiety, is impervious to Roger's coldness; he barely notices him. The word suggests a world of isolated individuals, each locked in their own impervious bubble. The mountain 'punches up a hole of blackness' in the star-filled sky, an image of negation, of absence. The impervious boys sit beneath that hole, unable to reach each other or the stars. Golding uses this word to diagnose the novel's central failure: the boys cannot break through to each other. Jack cannot hear Ralph; Ralph cannot hear Simon; Piggy cannot be heard by anyone. Their imperviousness is the true barrier, thicker than any jungle.
ex_en: Roger seemed impervious to the fear that gripped the other boys, his cold eyes watching and waiting for permission to hurt, a wall of stone where a heart should have been.
ex_zh: The old oak stood impervious to the storm, its roots a conversation with the centre of the earth, its leaves a shrug at the wind's tantrum.

def_en: an expression of sharp disapproval or criticism
def_zh: sharp rebuke, gentle rebuke, quiet rebuke
quote_en: "Piggy gave up the attempt to rebuke Ralph." – The narrator after Piggy tries to scold Ralph for being rude about Jack's hunters (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'rebuke' – a formal, often justified criticism – is attached to Piggy's failed attempt to correct Ralph. Piggy, who is always right, tries to rebuke Ralph for his lack of tact, but he 'gives up' because Ralph will not listen. This small moment encapsulates Piggy's tragedy: he sees clearly, he speaks truly, but his rebukes are always too weak, too late, or simply ignored. The word 'rebuke' implies a certain authority; one rebukes from a position of moral or social superiority. Piggy has the moral superiority but not the social standing. His rebukes are like a child scolding a parent – heard but not heeded. Golding uses this word to show that being right is not enough. On the island, authority comes from charisma, from physical presence, from the ability to intimidate or inspire. Piggy can rebuke, but he cannot enforce. His rebukes are the sighs of the powerless, the whispers of Cassandra.
ex_en: Ralph's silence was a rebuke sharper than any words, reminding Jack of the fire he had let go out, a quiet judgement that burned without smoke.
ex_zh: The teacher's gentle rebuke – 'Perhaps you could try again?' – was a velvet brick, soft to the touch but heavy with disappointment.

def_en: reserved, modest, or shy in manner, often with a suggestion of false modesty
def_zh: demure rows, demure smile, demure expression
quote_en: "Each of them wore the remains of a black cap and ages ago they had stood in two demure rows and their voices had been the song of angels." – The narrator remembering the choir boys as they once were, before they became hunters and savages (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'demure' – quiet, modest, conventionally well-behaved – describes the choir boys in their past life, standing in 'two demure rows' singing hymns. The word creates a sharp contrast with the present, where these same boys are 'demoniac figures with faces of white and red and green', howling and rushing with spears. The transformation from demure choirboys to savage hunters is the novel's central metamorphosis. The word 'demure' also carries a hint of performance: demure behaviour is often a social mask, learned and maintained for the approval of adults. Remove the adults, and the mask slips. Golding uses this word to suggest that civilisation is not a deep change but a surface, a costume that can be shed. The demure rows of the choir were always, underneath, a pack of hunters waiting for the right conditions to emerge.
ex_en: The demure choirboys who once sang hymns were now savages, their angelic voices replaced by bloodthirsty chants, their folded hands now clenched around spears.
ex_zh: She walked with a demure step, eyes down, but the boys watching knew that beneath the lace collar was a heart that beat for trouble.

def_en: intense and passionate feeling; zeal
def_zh: religious fervour, patriotic fervour, passionate fervour
quote_en: "If Jack was astonished by their fervor he did not show it." – The narrator after the hunters eagerly agree to forget the beast and follow Jack's leadership (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'fervour' – burning intensity, often associated with religious devotion – describes the hunters' response to Jack's proposal. They agree with a fervour that borders on worship. This is not reasoned assent; it is emotional surrender. Jack, who is 'astonished' (surprised by the strength of their response), wisely hides his surprise. He recognises that fervour is a tool, a force he can channel. The word has religious connotations: the tribe is becoming a congregation, and Jack its high priest. Their fervour will be expressed in the chanting, the dancing, the rituals of the kill. Golding uses this word to show how savagery recruits not through logic but through emotion. The fervour of belonging, of shared purpose, of ecstatic violence – these are more powerful than any argument for rescue. Piggy's logic cannot compete with the tribe's fervour.
ex_en: The boys followed Jack with a religious fervour, chanting his name and painting their faces in worship of savagery, each ritual a thread binding them to his will.
ex_zh: The crowd's fervour was a fever, their cheers a contagion, and by the time the speaker raised his fist, half of them could not remember why they had come.

def_en: behaving irrationally due to anger, distress, or mental illness; mad
def_zh: demented shriek, demented laughter, demented rage
quote_en: "One piglet, with a demented shriek, rushed into the sea trailing Roger's spear behind it." – The narrator during the hunt where the boys kill the sow, describing the piglet's terrified flight as Roger's spear drags behind it (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'demented' – from the Latin for 'out of one's mind' – transfers human madness to an animal. The piglet's shriek is not merely frightened; it is insane, broken by terror. This word choice blurs the line between human and animal suffering. The piglet's demented shriek foreshadows the demented screams of the human victims to come: Simon, crying out against the 'abominable noise'; Piggy, silent in his fall; Ralph, running for his life. The word also applies to the hunters themselves. Their excitement during the kill is a kind of dementia, a temporary madness that makes cruelty feel like joy. Golding uses 'demented' to suggest that violence drives both victim and perpetrator insane. The piglet's shriek is a prophecy of the tribe's moral collapse.
ex_en: The boys' demented dance around the fire, stabbing at Robert as if he were the pig, foreshadowed the murder to come, a rehearsal choreographed by madness.
ex_zh: The demented laughter of the asylum echoed down the corridor, a sound that remembered what sanity had forgotten.

def_en: became less interesting or enjoyable through familiarity; grew dull
def_zh: excitement palled, the game palled, the pleasure palled
quote_en: "At length even this palled." – The narrator after the boys had been laughing at Maurice's imitation of the pig during the celebration of the kill (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'palled' – from the same root as 'pale', meaning to fade or become bland – captures the rapid consumption of pleasure on the island. The boys' laughter at the pig imitation is intense but short-lived; even this amusement 'palled' because nothing sustains interest when there are no adults to enforce attention spans. The word suggests a kind of emotional hunger: the boys need ever stronger stimuli to feel anything. The hunt itself will soon pall for Jack; only the kill, the blood, the domination will suffice. Golding uses 'palled' to diagnose the psychology of addiction to violence. The first kill is thrilling; the second less so; by the time they hunt Ralph, the thrill is gone, replaced by mechanical cruelty. The word also applies to the conch, to the assemblies, to the hope of rescue: all pall, all fade, as the tribe descends further into savagery.
ex_en: The thrill of hunting palled for Jack; only the kill itself, the spilling of blood, could still satisfy him, a hunger that grew with every meal.
ex_zh: The carnival's bright lights palled after the third hour, the cotton candy losing its sugar, the laughter losing its luster.

def_en: fat and large in body; fleshy
def_zh: corpulent figure, corpulent body, corpulent frame
quote_en: "Then as the blue material of the parachute collapsed the corpulent figure would bow forward, sighing, and the flies settle once more." – The narrator describing the dead parachutist whose body Simon discovers on the mountain, swaying and bowing in the wind (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'corpulent' – a formal, almost clinical word for obesity – is applied to the dead man, whose body has swollen with decay. The word is grotesque in context: this is not a living fat man but a rotting corpse, the 'blue material' of the parachute collapsing and inflating like diseased lungs. The 'corpulent figure' bowing and sighing is the source of the boys' terror, the beast they have imagined. Golding uses this word to demystify the beast. It is not a monster with claws and teeth; it is a dead man, a 'corpulent' lump of decaying flesh. The horror is not supernatural but biological: the body that once contained a soul is now a puppet of the wind, a bag of meat and bone. The word 'corpulent' also connects to Piggy, whose 'ludicrous body' is similarly fat and vulnerable. The beast is Piggy's mirror: both are physical bodies that the tribe will destroy.
ex_en: The corpulent dead man on the mountain, swaying in the breeze, was the terrible truth behind the boys' imagined beast: not a monster, but a mirror.
ex_zh: The corpulent merchant shifted in his velvet chair, each breath a small wheeze, each wheeze a reminder of the feast that had made him.

def_en: contemptuous ridicule or mockery
def_zh: social derision, object of derision, howl of derision
quote_en: "Piggy once more was the center of social derision so that everyone felt cheerful and normal." – The narrator after Piggy is accidentally burned by the fire at Jack's feast, and the boys laugh at him (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'derision' – mocking contempt that excludes and humiliates – captures the social function of cruelty on the island. Piggy becomes the 'center of social derision', and this shared mockery makes 'everyone feel cheerful and normal'. The word is devastating: the boys' normality is built on Piggy's humiliation. They bond not through shared goals or affection but through a shared victim. Derision is the glue of the tribe. Golding uses this word to show how societies (including adult societies) often unite by excluding and mocking an outsider. Piggy is the scapegoat, the one whose pain confirms everyone else's belonging. The word also foreshadows his death: the derision that makes the boys cheerful is a rehearsal for the violence that will kill him. When the rock crushes Piggy, the derision ends – and so does the last chance for rescue.
ex_en: Piggy's every word was met with derision by the hunters, but his logic was the only thing that could have saved them, a treasure they threw away with their laughter.
ex_zh: The speaker's proposal was met with a wave of derision from the back benches, a tide of snickers that drowned the last lifeboat of reason.

def_en: walked in a slow, relaxed manner; strolled without hurry
def_zh: sauntered over, sauntered away, sauntered nonchalantly
quote_en: "Seeing there was no immediate likelihood of a pause, Jack rose from the log that was his throne and sauntered to the edge of the grass." – The narrator as Jack moves to confront Ralph and Piggy at the feast, his confident, unhurried walk expressing his power (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'sauntered' – a slow, casual, almost arrogant walk – captures Jack's assumption of authority. He is no longer the insecure boy who lost the election; he is a chief, and he moves like one. The word 'sauntered' contrasts with Ralph's 'limping' and Piggy's 'waddling'. Jack's body has become an instrument of dominance. He does not rush; he does not need to. His saunter is a performance of power, a message: I am in control, I am not threatened by you. The word also suggests a kind of theatricality. Jack is playing the role of chief, and his saunter is part of the costume. Golding uses this word to show how power is performed, not just exercised. The saunter is as important as the spear; the walk as meaningful as the kill.
ex_en: Jack sauntered through the camp like a king, his painted face and strutting confidence masking the boy he had once been, a costume that fit more tightly each day.
ex_zh: He sauntered into the room ten minutes late, as if the clock should have waited for him, and perhaps it did.

def_en: causing disgust or hatred; detestable, loathsome
def_zh: abominable noise, abominable act, abominable cruelty
quote_en: "It was crying out against the abominable noise something about a body on the hill." – The narrator describing Simon's final words as he tries to tell the boys the truth about the dead parachutist before they kill him, calling the chant of the hunters an 'abominable noise' (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'abominable' – from the Latin for 'to deprecate as an evil omen' – captures the moral quality of the hunters' chant. The noise is not just loud; it is evil, detestable, something that should not exist. Simon, in his dying moments, cries out 'against the abominable noise', his voice a last protest of civilisation against savagery. The word 'abominable' also echoes biblical language: the abomination of desolation, the abomination of the wicked. Golding uses this word to frame the murder of Simon as a kind of religious sacrifice gone horribly wrong. The boys are not merely killing; they are committing an abomination, a sin against humanity. Yet they do not know it. The word is Simon's judgement, but the tribe cannot hear him. The abominable noise drowns out the warning, and the abominable act is done.
ex_en: The abominable act of Simon's murder was never spoken of again by the tribe, but it stained their souls forever, a blood that no tide could wash away.
ex_zh: The abominable smell of burnt hair and something else, something worse, clung to the kitchen walls long after the fire was out.

def_en: to wave or flourish something, especially a weapon, as a threat or in display
def_zh: brandish a spear, brandish a knife, brandish a sword
quote_en: "Jack had him by the hair and was brandishing his knife." – The narrator during the mock hunt where the boys pretend to kill Robert, as Jack holds Robert's hair and waves his knife in the air (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'brandish' – a dramatic, often theatrical waving of a weapon – captures Jack's love of performance. He does not simply hold the knife; he brandishes it, showing it off, making sure everyone sees it. The word suggests a kind of play-acting that has become deadly serious. In the mock hunt, brandishing is still a game; by the end of the novel, Jack will brandish his spear at Ralph with murderous intent. The progression from mock brandishing to real brandishing is the novel's moral arc. Golding uses this word to show how violence is rehearsed, ritualised, practiced until it becomes instinct. The brandished knife is a symbol of power, but it is also a symbol of the emptiness of that power. Jack brandishes because he needs to be seen; his authority depends on spectacle.
ex_en: Jack would brandish his spear before the tribe, and they would cheer, hungry for the violence he promised, each flourish a promise of blood.
ex_zh: The knight brandished his sword before the dragon, the blade catching the sunset like a match about to strike.

def_en: in a way that shows sorrow, regret, or pity
def_zh: smiled ruefully, rubbed ruefully, shook his head ruefully
quote_en: "He rubbed his rump ruefully." – The narrator after Robert has been hurt during the mock hunt where the boys pretended to kill the pig, as he nurses his bruised backside (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's adverb 'ruefully' – expressing regret or sorrow, often with a touch of humour – captures Robert's mixed feelings. He is genuinely hurt (the mock hunt became too real), but he also understands that it was 'just a game'. The word suggests a recognition of pain without the dignity of serious grievance. Robert will not complain, will not demand justice; he will rub his rump ruefully and move on. This ruefulness is the response of the victim who has no power to change anything. It is the emotion of the powerless, the ones who are hurt but cannot protest. Golding uses this word to show how the tribe's casual violence is absorbed, normalised, shrugged off. Robert's ruefulness is a small tragedy: the moment when pain becomes routine. Later, when Piggy is killed, there is no rueful rubbing; there is only silence.
ex_en: Ralph looked ruefully at the darkening sky, knowing that another night without rescue meant another night of fear, a weight that grew heavier with every sunset.
ex_zh: He smiled ruefully at the broken vase, his apology already old before the pieces had stopped rolling.

def_en: a bold manner intended to impress or intimidate; pretended courage
def_zh: youthful bravado, foolish bravado, empty bravado
quote_en: "Ralph surprised himself, not so much by the quality of his voice, which was even, but by the bravado of its intention." – The narrator as Ralph insists they go to look for the beast on the mountain, surprised by his own boldness (Chapter 7)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'bravado' – showy courage that masks fear – captures Ralph's psychological state. He is afraid; he does not want to climb the mountain. But he says the words anyway, and the words themselves create a kind of courage. The 'bravado of its intention' means that Ralph's voice is performing bravery even if his heart is not. This is the difference between Ralph and Jack: Ralph's bravado is reluctant, self-aware, almost surprised; Jack's is automatic, unreflective, performed without irony. Golding uses 'bravado' to suggest that courage is not a fixed trait but a series of decisions, often made against one's own fear. Ralph's bravado carries him up the mountain, where he sees the beast (the dead parachutist) and runs. But he went; that is what matters. Bravado is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act despite it.
ex_en: Jack's bravado about hunting the beast hid a terror that only his painted mask could conceal, a panic that he drowned in the roar of the tribe.
ex_zh: The young soldier's bravado was a thin coat of paint over rust, and the first shell that landed near him stripped it all away.

def_en: saturated with liquid; soaked through
def_zh: sodden wood, sodden ground, sodden clothes
quote_en: "Much of it was damply rotten and full of insects that scurried; logs to be lifted from the soil with care or crumbled into sodden powder." – The narrator describing the wood the boys gather for the fire on the beach after Jack leaves, wood that is too wet to burn properly (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'sodden' – heavy with moisture to the point of uselessness – describes the dead wood that the boys must use for their new fire. The word suggests decay, weight, the opposite of the dry, flammable fuel they had on the mountain. This sodden wood will not burn well; the fire will be weak, the smoke thin. Golding uses 'sodden' as a metaphor for the boys' condition after Jack's departure. Their civilisation is sodden, waterlogged, unable to catch fire. The word also echoes the novel's climate: the rain, the humidity, the sweat that never dries. Everything on the island is sodden, including the boys' hopes. The contrast with the earlier, hopeful fire is stark. That fire, though destructive, was vigorous; this fire is sodden, pathetic, a damp match fizzling out. The word signals the decline of Ralph's leadership and the island's descent into permanent damp darkness.
ex_en: The sodden wreckage of the parachute lay tangled on the mountain, a forgotten sign of the world of grownups, rotting into the soil that would soon drink Simon's blood.
ex_zh: The sodden leaves clung to the forest floor like wet bandages, each step a small squelching confession of the rain's excess.

def_en: exaggerated or uncontrollable emotion, often among a group
def_zh: mass hysteria, wild hysteria, hysterical laughter
quote_en: "There was a suggestion of panic in the energy and hysteria in the cheerfulness." – The narrator describing the boys' mood as they work to build a new fire on the beach, noting that their apparent cheerfulness hides something desperate and unhinged (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'hysteria' – from the Greek for 'womb', historically associated with women but here describing the boys – captures the unstable emotional state of the group. Their 'cheerfulness' is not genuine joy but a manic, forced gaiety that covers 'panic'. This is the psychology of denial: if we laugh and work hard, we do not have to think about the beast, the fire, the lost boy. The word 'hysteria' suggests a collective breakdown, a shared madness that infects everyone. Golding uses this word to show how groups can lose touch with reality together. The tribe's later dancing, chanting, and murder are forms of hysteria – emotions so intense that they override individual judgement. Hysteria is the engine of the mob, the fuel of the kill. The boys' cheerful hysteria as they build the beach fire is a preview of the ecstatic hysteria that will kill Simon.
ex_en: The hysteria of the hunters' dance swept everyone up, until even Ralph found himself shouting the killing chant, his reason drowned in the drumbeat of the crowd.
ex_zh: The hysteria spread through the stadium like wildfire, a thousand voices screaming as one, the game forgotten, the goal irrelevant. Only the noise mattered.

def_en: a dark cloud or covering of smoke, dust, or gloom; a heavy layer
def_zh: pall of smoke, pall of silence, pall of darkness
quote_en: "A pall stretched for miles away from the island." – The narrator describing the massive cloud of smoke from the forest fire that raged out of control after the boys first lit it with Piggy's glasses (Chapter 2)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'pall' – a cloth draped over a coffin or a dark cloud of smoke – carries funereal connotations. The smoke is not just a cloud; it is a pall, a shroud covering the island and the sea beyond. This word choice transforms the boys' signal fire into a funeral pyre. The pall stretches 'for miles', suggesting that the destruction is vast, visible, inescapable. Notably, this is the first fire, the one that burns out of control and causes the disappearance of the littlun with the mulberry birthmark. The pall of smoke is both a sign of the boys' incompetence and a prophecy of the novel's tragic end. By the final chapter, the island itself will be covered in a pall of smoke from the fire Jack sets to hunt Ralph. The pall that began as an accident becomes, at the end, a deliberate weapon. Golding uses this word to link the boys' early mistakes to their later atrocities.
ex_en: A pall of fear hung over the shelters each night, as the littleuns whispered of the beast that came from the sea, a darkness that no fire could burn away.
ex_zh: The pall of volcanic ash turned noon to twilight, a grey blanket smothering the sun, the birds, the memory of blue.

def_en: based on illusion; not real; deceptive
def_zh: illusive appearance, illusive hope, illusive dream
quote_en: "There were no shadows under the trees but everywhere a pearly stillness, so that what was real seemed illusive and without definition." – The narrator describing the strange, oppressive atmosphere before the storm as Simon sits alone with the Lord of the Flies (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'illusive' – from the same root as 'illusion' – captures the breakdown of reality in Simon's vision. The pearly stillness makes the world seem unreal, undefined, as if the boundary between truth and hallucination has dissolved. This is the perfect setting for the Lord of the Flies, a talking pig's head that is both real (a physical object) and illusive (a product of Simon's feverish mind). The word 'illusive' suggests that the beast itself is illusive – not a tangible creature but a projection of the boys' fear. Yet illusions can kill. Simon's encounter with the illusive Lord of the Flies leads him to the truth about the dead parachutist, but that truth will not save him. Golding uses 'illusive' to question the nature of reality on the island. Is the beast real? Is the conch's power real? The novel's answer is that things become real when people believe in them, even if they are illusive.
ex_en: The island's beauty was illusive, for beneath the palm trees and blue flowers lay the capacity for murder, a paradise that was only a painted mask over a skull.
ex_zh: The oasis shimmered on the horizon, illusive as a promise, a pool of light that vanished when the thirsty man took one step closer.

def_en: to descend from the air and settle; to land or rest upon
def_zh: flies alight, birds alight, alight on a surface
quote_en: "Gorged, they alighted by his runnels of sweat and drank." – The narrator describing the flies that covered the pig's head and then settled on Simon's sweating body as he sits in the clearing (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'alighted' – a gentle, almost poetic word for landing – contrasts grotesquely with what the flies are doing. They are not delicate butterflies; they are bloated, gorged insects, fat with the blood of the pig's head. Yet they 'alight' on Simon's sweat as if they were guests at a banquet. The word creates a disturbing dissonance between form and content. Golding uses 'alight' to emphasise Simon's passivity, his acceptance of the flies' attention. He does not swat them; he lets them drink. This passivity foreshadows his death: Simon will not fight the tribe; he will simply try to tell them the truth, and they will kill him. The word also connects Simon to the natural world, to the cycles of feeding and decay that the other boys try to escape. Simon alights nowhere; he belongs to the island, and the island's creatures claim him.
ex_en: The flies would alight on the rotting pig's head, just as fear would alight on the boys' hearts at nightfall, a settling of dread that no torch could scatter.
ex_zh: The butterfly alighted on her outstretched palm, a scrap of stained glass that had chosen to rest, however briefly, on human flesh.

def_en: absorbing and integrating ideas, knowledge, or experience; taking in and understanding
def_zh: assimilating information, assimilating possibilities, slowly assimilating
quote_en: "Sitting on the tremendous rock in the torrid sun, Roger received this news as an illumination. He ceased to work at his tooth and sat still, assimilating the possibilities of irresponsible authority." – The narrator as Roger learns that Jack has tied up Wilfred for no reason, and Roger understands the power of arbitrary punishment (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'assimilating' – to absorb and make part of oneself – describes Roger's mental process as he grasps the concept of 'irresponsible authority'. The word suggests digestion: Roger is eating the idea, making it part of his being. This is a turning point in the novel. Up to this moment, Roger has been cruel but restrained, held back by the 'taboo of the old life'. Now he assimilates the possibility of violence without consequence. He will become the tribe's executioner, the boy who pushes the rock that kills Piggy, who sharpens a stick at both ends for Ralph. The word 'assimilating' implies a slow, deliberate process, but the change is instantaneous. Roger's assimilation of Jack's lawless authority is the moment his humanity ends. Golding uses this word to show that evil is not always a sudden explosion; sometimes it is a quiet absorption, a meal that poisons from within.
ex_en: Roger sat in silence, assimilating the dark pleasure of hurting others without consequence, a lesson Jack had taught him well, and one he would never unlearn.
ex_zh: The student sat at his desk, slowly assimilating the equations, each number a brick in a wall he was building between himself and the chaos of the world.

def_en: forced or driven to do something; obliged
def_zh: felt compelled, compelled by fear, compelled by duty
quote_en: "The mask compelled them." – The narrator after Jack paints his face for the first time, and the other hunters feel drawn to follow him, unable to resist the transformation (Chapter 4)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'compelled' – driven by an external force, without choice – captures the power of Jack's painted mask. The mask does not persuade or invite; it compels. The hunters cannot resist it; they are drawn into Jack's orbit by the sheer spectacle of his transformation. The word suggests a loss of agency, a hypnotic submission. This is how savagery recruits: not through argument but through compulsion, through the magnetism of the primitive. The mask compels because it frees Jack from shame and self-consciousness, and the hunters want that freedom too. Golding uses 'compelled' to diagnose the psychology of the mob. Individuals do not choose to join the tribe; they are compelled by forces they do not understand – fear, desire, the need to belong. The conch, by contrast, never compels. It only invites. The conch's weakness is its civility. Savagery compels; civilisation requests.
ex_en: Ralph felt compelled to blow the conch, even when he knew no one would come, because it was the last symbol of civilisation, a voice that refused to be silent even when no one listened.
ex_zh: She felt compelled to look back, though she knew the road behind her was as empty as the road ahead, a compulsion born of nothing but hope.

def_en: emitting or reflecting light; glowing; bright
def_zh: luminous glow, luminous wall, luminous eyes
quote_en: "Piggy sat expressionless behind the luminous wall of his myopia." – The narrator describing Piggy's appearance after Jack's raid that stole his glasses, as he sits with one remaining lens creating a strange, glowing barrier between him and the world (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'luminous' – full of light – is ironically applied to Piggy's blindness. The 'luminous wall of his myopia' is the bright blur of the world seen through his remaining shattered lens. The word 'luminous' usually suggests beauty, wonder, revelation. Here it suggests isolation. Piggy's luminous wall is a prison; it separates him from clear sight, from the other boys, from rescue. He cannot see without his glasses, and the tribe has taken them. Yet even in his helplessness, Piggy retains a kind of dignity. The luminous wall is all he has left, his last, fragile connection to the world. Golding uses this word to evoke the pathos of Piggy's situation. He is the novel's true light, its moral intelligence, but that light is trapped behind a lens, flickering, about to be extinguished. When the rock strikes him, the luminous wall shatters.
ex_en: The conch had once been a luminous object of power and order, but shattered, it became only worthless dust, a light that had gone out.
ex_zh: The luminous dial of the watch glowed in the dark tent, a green eye blinking每分钟, marking time that felt like it had stopped.

def_en: nearsightedness; inability to see distant objects clearly; also, lack of foresight
def_zh: severe myopia, moral myopia, intellectual myopia
quote_en: "Piggy sat expressionless behind the luminous wall of his myopia." – The narrator describing Piggy's physical blindness after losing one lens, but also suggesting his limited vision of the savage world around him (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'myopia' – a medical term for nearsightedness – describes Piggy's physical condition, but it also carries metaphorical weight. Myopia is the inability to see what is distant. Piggy cannot see the rock that will kill him; he cannot see the spear that Jack throws; he cannot see the faces of the painted savages. But his moral myopia is the opposite: he sees clearly the distant goal of rescue, but he cannot see the immediate danger. The word suggests that every perspective has its blindness. Piggy's myopia (physical) makes him vulnerable; his clarity (moral) makes him a prophet no one hears. The tribe, by contrast, has perfect physical sight but profound moral myopia. They see the pig, the meat, the dance – but they cannot see the consequences. Golding uses 'myopia' to ask a question that runs through the entire novel: who is truly blind?
ex_en: Jack's myopia was not physical but moral: he could see the pig, but not the rescue ship on the horizon, a nearsightedness of the soul that mistook blood for hope.
ex_zh: His myopia meant he saw the world through a blur, but sometimes the blur was kinder than the sharp edges others had to bear.

def_en: crying or whining with a runny nose; complaining in a feeble, self-pitying way
def_zh: sniveling child, sniveling apology, sniveling complaint
quote_en: "Piggy broke off, sniveling." – The narrator as Piggy demands that Ralph call an assembly to get his glasses back from Jack, overcome with frustration and despair (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'sniveling' – a weak, tearful, nasal kind of crying – captures Piggy at his most pathetic. The word is deliberately unflattering. Sniveling is not dignified grief; it is the helpless crying of a child who cannot control his body. Piggy, the novel's voice of reason, is reduced to sniveling because his glasses are gone, his ally Ralph is hesitating, and death is approaching. Golding uses this word to make us uncomfortable. We want Piggy to be brave, to stand up to Jack, to speak truth to power. Instead, he snivels. But this sniveling is not cowardice; it is the body's honest response to intolerable pressure. Piggy has been mocked, beaten, robbed, and now blind. His sniveling is his humanity showing through – the raw, undignified fact of his suffering. The other boys will kill him not because he is wrong but because his sniveling reminds them of their own weakness.
ex_en: The littleuns spent their first nights sniveling for their mothers, but soon even tears were forgotten in the dance of the tribe, the tribe that had no use for softness.
ex_zh: The child stood in the corner, sniveling into his sleeve, each wet sniff a protest against a world that had not given him what he wanted.

def_en: in a manner intended to appease or win favor; soothingly
def_zh: nodded propitiatingly, smiled propitiatingly, spoke propitiatingly
quote_en: "Piggy nodded propitiatingly. 'You're chief, Ralph. You remember everything.'" – Piggy trying to calm Ralph after Ralph becomes angry at the suggestion that he had forgotten about the smoke signal (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's adverb 'propitiatingly' – from the Latin for 'to make favourable', often used in religious contexts of appeasing angry gods – captures Piggy's desperate attempt to manage Ralph's temper. He is not merely agreeing; he is propitiating, offering a verbal sacrifice to calm the chief's wrath. The word suggests that Ralph has become a kind of deity, or at least a dangerously unpredictable authority. Piggy, who usually lectures Ralph, now flatters him. This reversal reveals how much the power dynamic has shifted. Ralph is on the edge of despair; Piggy is trying to hold him together with appeasement. The word 'propitiatingly' is rare and formal, which makes Piggy's grovelling seem almost ritualistic. He is performing loyalty because loyalty is all they have left. Golding uses this word to show how fear distorts relationships. Even the good characters begin to propitiate, to perform, to lie.
ex_en: Piggy spoke propitiatingly to Ralph, desperate to keep the last shred of alliance between them before they faced Jack, a bird singing to soothe a gathering storm.
ex_zh: The servant bowed propitiatingly, his eyes on the floor, his hands offering a tray of apologies in the form of small, dry cakes.

def_en: spoke or sang in a trembling voice; sounded unsteady with emotion
def_zh: voice quavered, quavered with fear, quavered in response
quote_en: "'Am I safe? I feel awful—'" quavered Piggy as they approached the Castle Rock to demand his glasses back (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'quavered' – a trembling, wavering voice, often from fear or age – captures Piggy's physical vulnerability as he walks toward his death. He cannot see; he is being led; he can hear the sea sucking at the rocks below. His voice quavers because his body knows what his mind cannot yet accept: he will not leave this place alive. The word 'quavered' suggests a musical instability, a note that cannot hold its pitch. Piggy's voice, which has been the novel's instrument of reason, now falters. He is no longer the whining, lecturing voice of the assembly; he is a frightened child asking for reassurance. Ralph does not answer. The quavering voice is the last sound Piggy makes before the rock falls. Golding uses this word to humanise Piggy in his final moment. He is not a symbol of reason; he is a boy, blind and terrified, his voice a trembling bridge between hope and silence.
ex_en: Ralph's voice never quavered when he called for rescue, but alone in the night, he wept like a baby, his silent sobs a tremble that no one heard.
ex_zh: Her voice quavered as she spoke the eulogy, each syllable a small bird trying to fly through a hurricane, every word a prayer against forgetting.

def_en: in an aggressively defiant or hostile manner; with savage eagerness to fight
def_zh: square up truculently, speak truculently, glare truculently
quote_en: "Truculently they squared up to each other but kept just out of fighting distance." – The narrator as Ralph and Jack face off near the Castle Rock, each unwilling to back down but also unwilling to strike first (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's adverb 'truculently' – from the Latin for 'fierce' or 'savage' – captures the mutual aggression between Ralph and Jack. They 'square up' like boxers, but they keep 'just out of fighting distance', a stalemate of hostility. The word suggests a performative violence, a posturing that waits for the other to make the first move. This is the last face-off before the novel's final violence. Ralph is still trying to negotiate; Jack is still playing the tyrant. Their truculence cannot last. Soon, Roger will push the rock, and the stalemate will break. Golding uses 'truculently' to describe the tension before catastrophe, the moment when two opposing forces are perfectly balanced and any small push will tip the world into chaos.
ex_en: Jack glared truculently at Ralph, daring him to take one step closer to the fortress of painted savages, his body a coiled spring, his spear a promise.
ex_zh: The child stood truculently before the broken vase, arms crossed, chin up, as if the shattered porcelain had attacked him first.

def_en: warded off a blow or attack; avoided answering a question
def_zh: parried the blow, parried the thrust, parried the question
quote_en: "Ralph sensed the position of the weapon from the glimpse he caught of Jack's arm and put the thrust aside with his own butt. Then he brought the end round and caught Jack a stinger across the ear." – The narrator describing Ralph parrying Jack's spear thrust and counterattacking during their fight at Castle Rock (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'parried' – a fencing term for deflecting an opponent's blade – applies the language of formal combat to the boys' crude spear fight. Ralph is not a trained fighter, but he manages to parry Jack's thrust, using the butt of his own spear to deflect the point. This moment shows that Ralph, despite his reluctance, can defend himself. He is not weak; he is civilised. The word 'parried' suggests skill, restraint, the opposite of Jack's wild aggression. Yet the parry is not enough. Ralph can parry Jack's spear, but he cannot parry Roger's rock, cannot parry the tribe's frenzy, cannot parry the fall of Piggy's body. The parry is a temporary defence against a system that will eventually overwhelm him. Golding uses this word to show that civilisation can deflect individual blows but cannot withstand a sustained assault on its foundations.
ex_en: Ralph parried Jack's spear, but he could not parry the realisation that all hope of rescue was gone, a blow that landed not on his body but on his soul.
ex_zh: She parried his question with a smile, a flick of the wrist that sent his curiosity spinning into the corner like a foil that had missed its mark.

def_en: an object believed to have magical powers; a charm that brings good luck or protection
def_zh: good luck talisman, magic talisman, sacred talisman
quote_en: "By him stood Piggy still holding out the talisman, the fragile, shining beauty of the shell." – The narrator as Piggy holds up the conch before Roger pushes the rock, the shell now a magical object that has lost its power (Chapter 11)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'talisman' – an object imbued with supernatural protection – elevates the conch from a simple shell to a sacred artefact. The word suggests that the boys have been treating the conch as a magical object, believing that its presence guarantees order and the right to speak. But a talisman only works if people believe in it. By this point in the novel, the tribe has stopped believing. The conch is 'fragile', 'shining', 'beautiful' – but it is also powerless. Piggy holds it out as a final, desperate plea to the old magic, but the tribe does not see magic; they see only a white shell in a blind boy's hands. The rock that shatters the conch also shatters the illusion that symbols have power. Golding uses 'talisman' to ask a painful question: what is the difference between a sacred object and a piece of rubbish? The answer: only belief.
ex_en: The conch was the boys' talisman against savagery, but when it shattered, so did their last defence, a broken charm on a beach of broken boys.
ex_zh: The old woman pressed the rabbit's foot into his palm – a talisman, she said, against the loneliness that had already eaten her heart.

def_en: believing that people are motivated by self-interest; distrustful of sincerity
def_zh: cynical laugh, cynical remark, cynical view
quote_en: "The half-shut eyes were dim with the infinite cynicism of adult life." – The narrator describing the skull of the pig's head that grins at Simon, the Lord of the Flies speaking with the weary, mocking wisdom of the adult world (Chapter 8)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'cynical' – a belief that human actions are selfish and insincere – is attributed to the pig's skull. The 'infinite cynicism of adult life' is the knowledge that the world is corrupt, that rescue will not come, that the beast is inside. The Lord of the Flies speaks with a cynical voice because it represents the truth that the boys cannot accept: that there is no monster to fight, only themselves. This cynicism is 'dim' – not bright or hopeful, but tired, resigned, aged. Simon, who sees the truth, is destroyed by it. The other boys prefer the excitement of the hunt to the cynical wisdom of the skull. Golding uses this word to suggest that adulthood is not wisdom but exhaustion, not hope but a weary acceptance of cruelty. The novel ends with the naval officer, a grownup, who arrives too late, his cynicism hidden behind his uniform.
ex_en: The Lord of the Flies seemed to grin with a cynical understanding that no rescue would ever come, a smirk that knew all the answers and refused to tell.
ex_zh: His cynical laugh was a door slamming on a conversation that had never been allowed to begin.

def_en: having a strong, bitter, sharp smell or taste; irritating
def_zh: acrid scent, acrid smoke, acrid taste
quote_en: "He rubbed his cheek along his forearm, smelling the acrid scent of salt and sweat and the staleness of dirt." – The narrator describing Ralph hiding in the thicket, aware of his own filth and decay as the tribe hunts him (Chapter 12)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'acrid' – a sharp, burning, unpleasant smell – captures Ralph's physical condition at the novel's end. He is no longer the fair-haired boy who first clambered onto the island; he is a creature of sweat, salt, and dirt. The 'acrid scent' is the smell of his own body, a reminder that he has become animal. This word also evokes the smoke of the fire Jack has set to flush him out, the acrid fumes that choke his lungs as he runs. Golding uses 'acrid' to link the physical and the moral. The acrid smell of Ralph's body is the smell of his desperation, his fear, his humanity reduced to its most basic elements. But it is also the smell of survival. Ralph is filthy, but he is alive. The acrid scent is the smell of one who has not yet given up.
ex_en: The acrid smoke from the burning forest filled Ralph's lungs, choking him as he ran for his life, a bitter taste that told him the island was dying too.
ex_zh: The acrid tang of burnt coffee clung to the kitchen, a ghost of mornings that had started too early and ended too late.

def_en: a line or ring of people or things surrounding an area; a barrier
def_zh: police cordon, sanitary cordon, cordon of hunters
quote_en: "If necessary, when the chase came too close, he could charge the cordon while it was still thin, burst through, and run back." – Ralph thinking about how to escape the tribe's systematic hunt for him, imagining a line of hunters advancing across the island (Chapter 12)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'cordon' – a military or police term for a line of people blocking access – applies the language of organised manhunts to the tribe's pursuit of Ralph. The word suggests that Jack's savages have become a disciplined force, no longer a chaotic mob but a methodical killing machine. The 'cordon' sweeps across the island, an inescapable net. Ralph's plan is to 'charge the cordon while it was still thin' – to exploit the weakness of the line before it tightens. This is a tactical, almost military calculation, far from the innocent games of the early chapters. Golding uses 'cordon' to show how far the boys have fallen. They are now engaged in a formal hunt, a military operation, with Ralph as the prey. The word also echoes the 'cordon sanitaire' – a barrier against disease. The tribe treats Ralph as a contagion to be isolated and destroyed.
ex_en: The tribe formed a cordon across the island, advancing methodically to flush Ralph out of hiding, a line of death drawing tighter with every step.
ex_zh: The police cordon fluttered with yellow tape, a ribbon of law stretched across the street, beyond which the ordinary world was not allowed to go.

def_en: howling or wailing loudly, especially as a ritual expression of grief or triumph
def_zh: ululating cry, ululating hunters, ululating chant
quote_en: "and the cordon waver on, ululating along the island" – The narrator imagining the hunters' advance as they search for Ralph, their high-pitched, wavering cries marking their progress (Chapter 12)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'ululating' – a long, wavering, high-pitched cry, often associated with mourning or celebration in various cultures – captures the hunters' terrifying chant. The word suggests ritual, ceremony, the formalisation of violence. The tribe does not simply shout; they ululate, a sound that is both primitive and organised. This ululation is the opposite of the conch's clear, commanding note. Where the conch summoned order, the ululation summons chaos. Golding uses this word to contrast two forms of collective sound: the civilised assembly and the savage hunt. The ululating cordon sweeps across the island, a wave of sound that flushes Ralph from his hiding place. By the end, the conch is silent, and only the ululation remains.
ex_en: The ululating voices of the hunters grew louder as they closed in on Ralph's hiding place in the thicket, a chorus of wolves that had forgotten they were ever boys.
ex_zh: The ululating cry of the ambulance cut through the city's thick silence, a siren that mourned each second it took to arrive.

def_en: very large or clumsy, like an elephant; massive and ponderous
def_zh: elephantine progress, elephantine size, elephantine proportions
quote_en: "Then the red thing was past and the elephantine progress diminished toward the sea." – The narrator after the great rock pushed by Roger crashes past Ralph's hiding place, the boulder's massive, slow roll toward the ocean (Chapter 12)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'elephantine' – huge, slow, and heavy – describes the boulder that Roger has released. The word evokes an elephant's lumbering gait: unstoppable but ponderous, weighty beyond human scale. This 'elephantine progress' is the opposite of the quick, darting movements of the hunters. It is the slow, inevitable hand of gravity, the physics of destruction. The boulder kills Piggy not because Roger aimed well but because it was too massive to avoid. Golding uses 'elephantine' to suggest that violence, once unleashed, takes on a life of its own, becoming a force of nature as indifferent and unstoppable as an elephant stampede. The word also contrasts with the smallness of the boys. Against elephantine forces – the ocean, the fire, the rock – their struggles are pitiful. The elephantine rock rolls on, and Piggy is crushed, and the conch is shattered, and there is nothing any boy can do.
ex_en: The elephantine rock that killed Piggy was pushed by a single savage hand, proving how easily power destroys reason when reason weighs nothing.
ex_zh: The elephantine tank rumbled down the narrow street, its treads chewing cobblestones into dust, a dinosaur in a world that had forgotten how to hide.

def_en: a crackling or rattling sound; a series of sharp noises
def_zh: loud crepitation, crepitation of flames, crepitation of dry leaves
quote_en: "He heard a curious trickling sound and then a louder crepitation as if someone were unwrapping great sheets of cellophane." – The narrator as Ralph hears the savages setting the thicket on fire to smoke him out, the crackling of the flames growing louder (Chapter 12)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'crepitation' – a technical term for crackling sounds, often used in medicine (joint crepitation) or physics – gives a clinical precision to the sound of the fire. The word is oddly formal for such a primal, destructive sound. This formality creates a sense of detachment, as if Ralph's mind is trying to distance itself from the terror by naming it precisely. The crepitation is the sound of his hiding place burning, of the island being consumed, of his last chance at safety turning to ash. Golding uses 'crepitation' to make the familiar sound of fire strange and threatening. The word also connects to the earlier 'drum-roll' of the fire, but where the drum-roll was rhythmic and almost musical, the crepitation is sharp, random, panicked – the sound of a world breaking apart.
ex_en: The crepitation of the fire behind him urged Ralph forward, faster than he had ever run before, each crackle a bite on his heels.
ex_zh: The crepitation of the old radio was the only voice in the empty house, a static conversation between the living and the dead.

def_en: ornamental shoulder pieces on a uniform, especially a military one
def_zh: gold epaulettes, officer's epaulettes, braided epaulettes
quote_en: "He saw white drill, epaulettes, a revolver, a row of gilt buttons down the front of a uniform." – The narrator as Ralph looks up at the naval officer who has arrived to rescue them, seeing the symbols of adult authority and civilisation (Chapter 12)
quote_zh: Golding's noun 'epaulettes' – the decorative shoulder ornaments on military uniforms – represents the adult world that Ralph has been desperately trying to reach. The word is specific, detailed, almost fetishistic in its attention to the officer's uniform. Ralph, who has been reduced to a filthy, half-naked savage, sees these epaulettes as a vision of order, power, rescue. But the irony is crushing. The officer's epaulettes belong to a warship; he is part of the same adult civilisation that dropped the atom bomb, that fights wars, that produces the 'infinite cynicism' of the Lord of the Flies. The epaulettes are a costume, a performance of authority. They mean nothing to the dead parachutist on the mountain, nothing to the boy with the mulberry birthmark, nothing to Piggy's crushed body. Golding uses 'epaulettes' to ask: what is the difference between Jack's painted mask and the officer's uniform? Both are costumes; both conceal the same darkness.
ex_en: The naval officer's epaulettes gleamed in the firelight, a symbol of the adult world that had arrived too late, a costume that could not hide the warship behind it.
ex_zh: The general's epaulettes caught the sun like golden fish scales, each thread a medal for a war that someone else had fought.

def_en: to absorb and integrate into the mind or a larger group; to take in and understand fully
def_zh: assimilate information, assimilate into a culture, assimilate new ideas
quote_en: "Sitting on the tremendous rock in the torrid sun, Roger received this news as an illumination. He ceased to work at his tooth and sat still, assimilating the possibilities of irresponsible authority." – The narrator as Roger realizes that Jack has tied up Wilfred for no reason, and Roger begins to understand the power of arbitrary punishment (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: Golding's verb 'assimilate' – to absorb and make part of oneself – describes Roger's mental process as he grasps the concept of 'irresponsible authority'. The word suggests digestion: Roger is eating the idea, making it part of his being. This is a turning point in the novel. Up to this moment, Roger has been cruel but restrained, held back by the 'taboo of the old life'. Now he assimilates the possibility of violence without consequence. He will become the tribe's executioner, the boy who pushes the rock that kills Piggy, who sharpens a stick at both ends for Ralph. Golding uses 'assimilate' to show that evil is not always a sudden explosion; sometimes it is a quiet absorption, a meal that poisons from within.
ex_en: The boys quickly assimilated the savage rituals of the tribe, forgetting the civilized rules they had learned at home, each new chant a meal that erased an old memory.
ex_zh: The immigrant children assimilated the new language like rain into thirsty soil, but their mother's accent remained a stubborn root that would not dissolve.

def_en: clumsy or awkward in movement; not graceful
def_zh: ungainly feet, ungainly gait, ungainly posture
quote_en: "The parachute took the figure forward, furrowing the lagoon, and bumped it over the reef and out to sea. The figure slid, rose to its feet, spun, swayed down through a vastness of wet air and trod with ungainly feet the tops of the high trees; falling, still falling, it sank toward the beach and the boys rushed screaming into the darkness." – The narrator describing the dead parachutist being carried out to sea after the storm, his body moving in clumsy, puppet-like motions (Chapter 9)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'ungainly' – clumsy, awkward, lacking grace – describes the dead parachutist's movements as the wind animates his corpse. The word is grotesque because it applies human awkwardness to a dead thing. An ungainly living person stumbles; an ungainly corpse is a horror. The parachutist's 'ungainly feet' tread the tops of the trees, a macabre dance that the boys mistake for the beast. Golding uses 'ungainly' to emphasise the contrast between the boys' imagined monster (powerful, terrifying, intentional) and the reality (a pathetic, clumsy, accidental corpse). The ungainliness of the dead man is the novel's final irony: the beast that inspired so much terror was nothing but a bumbling, rotting body, a puppet of the wind.
ex_en: The ungainly dance of the dead parachutist in the wind had convinced the boys that a beast haunted the mountain, a horror born not of claws but of clumsiness.
ex_zh: The young giraffe rose on ungainly legs, a tower of joints and angles that somehow learned to run before it learned to stand.

def_en: looking sad, miserable, or sorrowful; afflicted with woe
def_zh: woebegone expression, woebegone child, woebegone figure
quote_en: "His twitchings exhausted him till he lay, breathless and woebegone, waiting for the next spasm." – The narrator after Ralph's uncontrollable sniggering subsides, as he lies exhausted and miserable after the fight with Jack's raiders (Chapter 10)
quote_zh: Golding's adjective 'woebegone' – from 'woe' and 'begone', meaning surrounded by sorrow – captures Ralph's state after the raid. He is not merely tired; he is woebegone, a word that suggests a visible, almost theatrical sadness. The twitching that exhausted him was laughter – a dark, hysterical laughter at Piggy's joke about writing to his auntie. Now the laughter is gone, replaced by the weight of reality: the fire is dead, the glasses are stolen, and one of the twins has a loose tooth from the fight. Ralph's woebegone expression is the face of defeated leadership. He has tried to hold civilisation together, and he has failed. The word 'woebegone' evokes pity, not respect. Ralph is no longer the confident chief; he is a miserable, exhausted boy, abandoned by almost everyone.
